caveats: a: No six-digit occupation matrix or direct custom-framing AI exposure study was found.
a: The supplied compensation-to-receipts input is LOW quality at ancestor 44-45 with a 2024-wage/2022-receipts mismatch and may be especially misleading for a craft-heavy subset.
a: Visual generation assists customer decisions but does not eliminate fabrication labor.
rho: Gallup's frontline adoption evidence spans all industries rather than frame shops.
rho: The estimate assumes exposed tasks are not already automated and excludes revenue lift.
e: The official NAICS examples do not reveal establishment shares by activity.
e: PPFA confirms an organized professional segment but publishes no denominator or LMM eligibility count on the cited page.
e: The supplied n input is missing, so the share cannot yield a defensible target count.
s5: The Gallup sample is cross-industry and reports plans, not completed transfers.
s5: No custom-framing control-transfer series was found.
s5: Owner-dependent craft shops may close or sell assets rather than transfer an operating company.
q: No direct study measures automation-benefit pass-through in custom framing.
q: Trade accounts bargain and rebid differently from sentimental one-off residential buyers.
q: General retail e-commerce growth is competitive context, not framing-specific price elasticity.
d5: The forecast is commercial, North American, and broader than the chosen service basket.
d5: Revenue growth is not quantity growth and may include inflation or premium mix.
d5: Demand is exposed to discretionary spending, commercial interiors, and art-market cycles.
o: O*NET is for retail salespersons generally, not professional framers.
o: The e-commerce statistic covers all retail and does not isolate custom framing.
o: Online platforms may remain accountable operators even when local storefronts are displaced.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Fixed-price custom projects combining materials markup with design and skilled fabrication labor; repeat revenue comes from professional trade, gallery, hospitality, corporate, and institutional accounts rather than subscriptions.
lens.deviation_from_default: NAICS 449129 explicitly combines unrelated retail concepts including kitchenware, linen, lamps, glassware, custom picture frames, and wood-burning stoves. These have incompatible work, demand, and operator requirements, so the lens is narrowed to custom picture-framing retailers, the code's clearly service-bearing craft segment, and then to firms with repeat professional or institutional customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Kitchenware, china, linen, bath, lamp, glassware, housewares, and wood-stove retailers; commodity ready-made frame sellers; pure online print-and-frame platforms; captive gallery workshops; hobby operators; and owner-only craft shops without transferable staff or processes.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market custom picture-framing retailers that repeatedly provide design, conservation-oriented specification, fabrication, mounting, delivery, installation, and project coordination to galleries, designers, artists, hospitality operators, corporate art programs, museums, and other repeat external clients.

a.bridge: BLS measures the broader furniture and home-furnishings retail occupation mix, while O*NET describes retail-sales tasks and Anthropic measures occupation-level LLM coverage rather than custom-framing wage-weighted tasks. I map customer intake, option retrieval, visual mockups, quoting support, communications, order records, marketing, and bookkeeping to exposed work, but leave physical design judgment, art handling, cutting, joining, mounting, finishing, delivery, and installation largely unexposed. The broad retail mix probably understates fabrication labor in framing shops and therefore tends to overstate exposure.
caveats: No six-digit occupation matrix or direct custom-framing AI exposure study was found.
The supplied compensation-to-receipts input is LOW quality at ancestor 44-45 with a 2024-wage/2022-receipts mismatch and may be especially misleading for a craft-heavy subset.
Visual generation assists customer decisions but does not eliminate fabrication labor.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task diary from at least 25 independent custom-frame shops, separated by designer, framer, installer, sales, and administrative roles and tested against current multimodal AI, would materially move the range.
a.rationale: Custom framing contains a useful information layer around a physical craft. AI can accelerate mockups, product and conservation guidance retrieval, quote preparation, client follow-up, and back-office work, but most production labor and final aesthetic or preservation accountability remains human.

d5.bridge: A commercial forecast projects 4.4% annual North American picture-frame market revenue growth from 2025 to 2033, but it covers standard, custom, digital, online, and offline frames across the U.S. and Canada and is nominal revenue rather than constant-price service quantity. I remove inflation and much of the product/channel mix effect, then widen for discretionary decor and art-market cyclicality. The bridge may overstate custom-service volume if digital or standard frames drive growth and understate it if professional framing gains share.
caveats: The forecast is commercial, North American, and broader than the chosen service basket.
Revenue growth is not quantity growth and may include inflation or premium mix.
Demand is exposed to discretionary spending, commercial interiors, and art-market cycles.
d5.change_evidence: U.S. custom-framing order counts and constant-quality realized prices by residential, gallery, designer, hospitality, corporate, and museum channel from 2024 through 2031 would materially move the ratio.
d5.rationale: Artwork, photographs, memorabilia, hospitality refreshes, exhibitions, and corporate interiors continue to require framing, but the service is discretionary and durable completed pieces need infrequent replacement. Stable to modestly growing real quantity is plausible, with downturn and online-channel risks.

caveats: The official NAICS examples do not reveal establishment shares by activity.
PPFA confirms an organized professional segment but publishes no denominator or LMM eligibility count on the cited page.
The supplied n input is missing, so the share cannot yield a defensible target count.
e.change_evidence: A matched Census microdata or PPFA-directory study coding every 449129 employer by primary activity, revenue, EBITDA, staff, client mix, repeat-account share, and owner dependence would directly identify the eligible proportion.
e.rationale: Custom picture framing is only one activity inside a residual home-furnishings code that lists many unrelated retailer types. The lens further requires LMM scale, repeat professional or institutional accounts, transferable employees and processes, and external service accountability, excluding both commodity retailers and owner-only craft shops. No defensible firm census by these traits was found.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 44-45 ($651,293,900,629) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 44-45 ($6,850,896,411,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: ancestor 44-45. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1011 (margins.json[44]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Retail (General) EBITDA/Sales 10.11%); revenue/employee $430,267 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 44-45); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

o.bridge: O*NET shows retail selling is intensely customer-facing and includes selection, product explanation, estimating, packaging, delivery/service arrangement, and physical handling, while Census reports e-commerce at 16.9% of total retail sales and growing faster than total retail. I infer that bespoke design, preservation choices, irregular-object handling, fabrication, and installation continue to require an accountable frame-shop operator, while digital images, standardized sizes, online visualization, mail-in framing, ready-made frames, and DIY remove part of the quantity. General retail e-commerce can overstate displacement of fragile or valuable physical works but understate it for digital-photo orders.
caveats: O*NET is for retail salespersons generally, not professional framers.
The e-commerce statistic covers all retail and does not isolate custom framing.
Online platforms may remain accountable operators even when local storefronts are displaced.
o.change_evidence: Channel-specific custom-framing orders showing online versus local fulfillment, standard versus irregular objects, professional fabrication content, mail-in damage/return rates, and installation attach would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: AI and software can self-serve visualization and configuration, but they cannot fully receive, preserve, cut, join, mount, finish, deliver, and install unique physical objects. The operator-required share remains material for professional and conservation work, though routine photo and standard-frame orders can move online.

caveats: No direct study measures automation-benefit pass-through in custom framing.
Trade accounts bargain and rebid differently from sentimental one-off residential buyers.
General retail e-commerce growth is competitive context, not framing-specific price elasticity.
q.change_evidence: A shop-level panel connecting implemented AI labor savings to realized gross margin, discounting, bid win rates, trade-account repricing, remake cost, and client retention over five years would materially change retention.
q.rationale: Fixed project pricing lets a custom framer retain early productivity gains, and conservation knowledge, craftsmanship, art-handling trust, and reliable turnaround reduce immediate price comparison. Over five years, online configurators, chains, supplier tools, professional-client negotiation, and local price competition share the gain through lower quotes or higher service levels.

caveats: Gallup's frontline adoption evidence spans all industries rather than frame shops.
The estimate assumes exposed tasks are not already automated and excludes revenue lift.
rho.change_evidence: Before-and-after telemetry from point-of-sale and visualization vendors serving custom framers, including adoption, rework, quote time, conversion, labor hours, and error rates over two years, would materially change this estimate.
rho.rationale: Packaged visual-assistance, CRM, messaging, quote, purchasing, and bookkeeping tools are implementable within five years. Realization is capped by small-shop systems, fragmented moulding and glazing catalogs, limited structured data, bespoke exceptions, preservation risk, employee training, and the need to verify every specification before materials are cut.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures owner intentions across U.S. small businesses: 14% planned to sell, go public, or transfer ownership within five years, 17% among owners age 55 or older, and 22% among employer-business owners. I center a scaled employer frame shop above the all-owner rate but below the employer intention rate after excluding gifts, public offerings, internal reorganizations, closures, and shops whose craft or client relationships do not survive the owner. This proxy may overstate completed qualifying sales because intentions fail and may understate pressure in an owner-aged craft trade.
caveats: The Gallup sample is cross-industry and reports plans, not completed transfers.
No custom-framing control-transfer series was found.
Owner-dependent craft shops may close or sell assets rather than transfer an operating company.
s5.change_evidence: Closed-transaction data from BizBuySell, Axial, PPFA members, framing franchises, and specialist brokers, screened for custom-framing employer firms and continuing control transfers, would materially change the probability.
s5.rationale: A staffed shop with a repeat trade book, equipment, premises, vendor relationships, and trained framers is transferable, but bespoke customer trust and owner design skill can be difficult to separate. General succession evidence indicates a material but not dominant five-year flow.

principal_driver: A defendable physical craft and professional client trust preserve operator need while AI reduces the surrounding design-support and administrative burden.
principal_weakness: The recurring, scaled custom-framing subset appears very small and is not separately measured within an unusually heterogeneous residual retail code.

sources.supports: NAICS 449129 retails home furnishings other than furniture, floor coverings, and window treatments and lists bath, kitchenware, china, linen, lamp, custom picture-frame, glassware, wood-stove, and housewares retailers as examples.
sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: All Other Home Furnishings Retailers

sources.supports: In broader furniture and home-furnishings retail, BLS reports 143,180 retail salespersons, 25,210 cashiers, 24,660 retail supervisors, 23,850 material movers, 20,300 general managers, and smaller driving, stocking, office, customer-service, and bookkeeping groups.
sources.title: May 2024 OEWS Industry Employment Charts

sources.supports: Retail sales tasks include customer-needs discovery, recommendation, pricing, product explanation, special orders, records, contracts, cost estimates, packaging, and service or delivery arrangements; physical handling and face-to-face interaction are also material.
sources.title: O*NET: Retail Salespersons

sources.supports: Anthropic measures exposure from O*NET tasks, theoretical LLM speedup, actual work usage, automation mode, and time weights, and reports high coverage in customer-service and data-entry work while many physical roles have no observed coverage.
sources.title: Labor market impacts of AI: A new measure and early evidence

sources.supports: Gallup reports frequent AI use of 9% among production and frontline workers in 2025, versus 27% among white-collar employees, illustrating adoption constraints in physical frontline settings.
sources.title: AI Use at Work Has Nearly Doubled in Two Years

sources.supports: PPFA maintains a professional directory and Certified Picture Framer and Master Certified Picture Framer programs, confirming an organized professional custom-framing segment.
sources.title: Professional Picture Framers Association

sources.supports: The commercial forecast values the North American picture-frame market at $3.845 billion in 2024 and projects 4.4% annual revenue growth from 2025 through 2033 across standard, custom, and digital frames.
sources.title: North America Picture Frame Market Size, Share & Trends

sources.supports: Census reports e-commerce at 16.9% of total U.S. retail sales in first-quarter 2026, growing 9.8% year over year versus 3.9% for all retail.
sources.title: Quarterly Retail E-Commerce Sales, First Quarter 2026

sources.supports: Gallup found 14% of owners planned to sell, go public, or transfer within five years, 17% among owners 55 or older, and 22% among employer-business owners; intentions include nonqualifying transfer forms.
sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan

title: All Other Home Furnishings Retailers
